expectations.text: A test file exists and contains at least 4 tests covering plain number, multiple of 3, multiple of 5, and multiple of 15
expectations.evidence: test_fizzbuzz.py exists with 8 tests. The 4 forcing tests cover: fizzbuzz(1)=="1" (plain number), fizzbuzz(3)=="Fizz" (multiple of 3), fizzbuzz(5)=="Buzz" (multiple of 5), fizzbuzz(15)=="FizzBuzz" (multiple of 15). All confirmed passing.

expectations.text: The production code file exists and all tests pass
expectations.evidence: fizzbuzz.py exists with a complete implementation. Running pytest confirmed all 8 tests pass: 8 passed in 0.01s.

expectations.text: The first test written is for a plain number (not the full FizzBuzz case), showing simplest-first ordering
expectations.evidence: Cycle 1 writes test_returns_number_as_string_for_non_multiple asserting fizzbuzz(1)=="1" — a plain number. The FizzBuzz case (fizzbuzz(15)) is not tackled until Cycle 4.

expectations.text: No production code exists that is not covered by at least one test
expectations.evidence: All four branches in fizzbuzz.py are exercised: the combined `n%3==0 and n%5==0` branch by fizzbuzz(15) and fizzbuzz(30), the `n%3==0` branch by fizzbuzz(3) and fizzbuzz(6), the `n%5==0` branch by fizzbuzz(5) and fizzbuzz(10), and `return str(n)` by fizzbuzz(1) and fizzbuzz(2).

expectations.text: Forcing tests (plain number returning itself, first Fizz, first Buzz, first FizzBuzz) appear before any triangulation tests (e.g. Fizz(6), Buzz(10), FizzBuzz(30))
expectations.evidence: In test_fizzbuzz.py lines 4–17, the four forcing tests appear first. The triangulation tests (fizzbuzz(2), fizzbuzz(6), fizzbuzz(10), fizzbuzz(30)) are grouped after line 20 with the comment '# --- Triangulation tests ---'. The transcript confirms triangulation tests were only written after all forcing cycles were complete.

expectations.text: Test names describe the behaviour being verified rather than just the input value (e.g. 'returns_fizz_for_multiple_of_3' rather than 'test_3')
expectations.evidence: All test names are behavioural: test_returns_number_as_string_for_non_multiple, test_returns_fizz_for_multiple_of_3, test_returns_buzz_for_multiple_of_5, test_returns_fizzbuzz_for_multiple_of_both_3_and_5, and equivalent descriptive names for triangulation tests. No test is named merely by its input value.

expectations.text: The implementation evolves through at least two distinct transformation steps (e.g. constant return before conditional logic is introduced)
expectations.evidence: Cycle 1 GREEN uses TPP #2 (return constant "1"). Cycle 2 GREEN applies TPP #5 (add conditional n%3==0). Cycle 3 adds a second conditional. Cycle 4 adds a combined conditional. The refactor then replaces "1" with str(n). At minimum three distinct transformations are documented.

expectations.text: The refactor phase is explicitly considered; opportunities such as combining the modulo checks or removing duplication are noted or applied

After Cycle 4, the refactor is applied: replacing 'return "1"' with 'return str(n)', documented as 'a hardcoded constant that happens to satisfy fizzbuzz(1)=="1" but does not express the intent.' All four cycles include explicit refactor consideration.

expectations.text: A test for the combined vip=True and code='SAVE10' case is written and confirmed to fail before any fix is applied
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 1 shows test_vip_and_save10_discount_applied_once added and run before any fix, producing 'AssertionError: assert 80.0 == 90.0' with 1 failed, 3 passed.

expectations.text: The fix is minimal — only the double-discount logic is changed
expectations.evidence: discount.py shows only the two independent `if` blocks were merged into `if/elif`. No other code in the function was altered.

expectations.text: All tests pass after the fix
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 2 shows '4 passed in 0.01s' with all four tests including the new regression test marked PASSED.

expectations.text: No unrelated behaviour was changed alongside the fix
expectations.evidence: The only change in discount.py is the second `if` becoming `elif`. The three pre-existing tests (no discount, vip-only, SAVE10-only) all still pass, confirming existing behaviour is preserved.

expectations.text: The regression test name clearly identifies the bug scenario being reproduced
expectations.evidence: Test is named `test_vip_and_save10_discount_applied_once` — names both triggering conditions (vip + SAVE10) and the defect (applied once vs twice).

expectations.text: The regression test contains a single focused assertion that directly captures the double-discount defect
expectations.evidence: test_discount.py line 14 has exactly one assertion: `assert calculate_discount(100, vip=True, code='SAVE10') == 90.0`, directly checking that the combined case yields 10% off (not 20%).

expectations.text: The fix does not introduce new complexity or branches beyond what is needed to prevent the double application
expectations.evidence: The fix converts `if/if` to `if/elif` — one keyword change, no new branches, conditions, variables, or helper functions introduced.

expectations.text: A test for the combined vip=True and code='SAVE10' case is written and confirmed to fail before any fix is applied
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 1 shows `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount` added and the failing output: `FAILED test_discount.py::test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount — AssertionError: assert 80.0 == 90.0` with `1 failed, 3 passed in 0.05s` before any fix.

expectations.text: The fix is minimal — only the double-discount logic is changed
expectations.evidence: discount.py replaces the two additive `discount += 0.10` branches with a single `if vip or code == 'SAVE10': discount = 0.10 else: discount = 0.0`. The return statement and function signature are unchanged. Nothing else was touched.

expectations.text: All tests pass after the fix
expectations.evidence: Transcript Step 2 shows all 4 tests passing: `test_no_discount_for_regular_customer PASSED`, `test_vip_gets_10_percent_discount PASSED`, `test_save10_code_gets_10_percent_discount PASSED`, `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount PASSED` — `4 passed in 0.01s`.

expectations.text: No unrelated behaviour was changed alongside the fix
expectations.evidence: The three pre-existing tests (no discount, vip-only, SAVE10-only) all continue to pass after the fix, confirming their behaviour is preserved. Only the double-discount path was altered.

expectations.text: The regression test name clearly identifies the bug scenario being reproduced
expectations.evidence: Test is named `test_vip_with_save10_code_gets_only_10_percent_discount` — this explicitly encodes the inputs (vip + SAVE10 code) and the expected outcome (only 10% discount), unambiguously identifying the bug scenario.

expectations.text: The regression test contains a single focused assertion that directly captures the double-discount defect
expectations.evidence: test_discount.py line 14: exactly one assertion — `assert calculate_discount(100, vip=True, code='SAVE10') == 90.0` — which directly tests the combined case and catches the 80.0 vs 90.0 double-discount defect.

expectations.text: The fix does not introduce new complexity or branches beyond what is needed to prevent the double application
expectations.evidence: The buggy code had two separate `if` statements (2 branches). The fix collapses them into one `if/else` using `or` (still 2 branches but simpler — no accumulation variable mutation). No new conditions, flags, or guard clauses were added.

expectations.text: The first test is for an empty list returning 0
expectations.evidence: Cycle 1 RED writes `def test_empty_list_returns_zero(): assert sum_list([]) == 0` — exactly an empty-list → 0 test.

expectations.text: The implementation for the empty-list test returns the constant 0, not a general loop
expectations.evidence: Cycle 1 GREEN implementation is `def sum_list(lst): return 0` — a bare constant, no loop or branching.

expectations.text: A second test for a single-element list forces a more general approach
expectations.evidence: Cycle 2 RED adds `test_single_element_returns_that_element` (`sum_list([5]) == 5`), which fails the constant-0 implementation and forces the conditional `if not lst: return 0; return lst[0]`.

expectations.text: Iteration or equivalent is only introduced once a two-or-more-element test forces it
expectations.evidence: Iteration (`for n in lst: total += n`) appears only in Cycle 3 GREEN, triggered by `test_multiple_elements_returns_their_sum` with `[1,2,3]`. Cycles 1 and 2 use no loop.

expectations.text: All tests pass with the final implementation
expectations.evidence: Transcript shows `3 passed in 0.01s` after the Cycle 3 refactor; live pytest run also confirms all 3 tests pass.

expectations.text: The refactor phase explicitly considers replacing hand-written iteration with the built-in sum()
expectations.evidence: Cycle 3 REFACTOR section explicitly states: 'The hand-written total=0; for n in lst: total+=n; return total is exactly what Python's built-in sum() does' and replaces it with `return sum(lst)`.

expectations.text: Test names describe the behavioural rule being tested (e.g. 'empty_list_sums_to_zero', 'single_element_list_sums_to_that_element') rather than just the inputs
expectations.evidence: Test names are `test_empty_list_returns_zero`, `test_single_element_returns_that_element`, `test_multiple_elements_returns_their_sum` — all express a behavioural rule, not merely raw inputs.

expectations.text: The final implementation is O(n) — no nested iteration or quadratic behaviour is introduced
expectations.evidence: Final implementation is `return sum(lst)` — a single O(n) pass over the list with no nested loops or quadratic operations.

expectations.text: The first test is for an empty list returning 0
expectations.evidence: Step 1 in transcript writes `test_empty_list()` asserting `sum_list([]) == 0` as the first test.

expectations.text: The implementation for the empty-list test returns the constant 0, not a general loop
expectations.evidence: Step 1 minimal implementation is `return 0` — a constant, not a loop.

expectations.text: A second test for a single-element list forces a more general approach
expectations.evidence: Step 2 introduces `test_single_element()` asserting `sum_list([5]) == 5`, which causes the implementation to be extended to a loop accumulator.

expectations.text: Iteration or equivalent is only introduced once a two-or-more-element test forces it
expectations.evidence: The loop is introduced in Step 2 (single-element test), not deferred until Step 3 (two-element test). A single-element test could still be satisfied with `return numbers[0] if numbers else 0` without iteration. The two-element test was not the trigger for iteration.

expectations.text: All tests pass with the final implementation
expectations.evidence: Test output shows '7 passed in 0.01s' covering all 7 tests.

expectations.text: The refactor phase explicitly considers replacing hand-written iteration with the built-in sum()
expectations.evidence: There is no explicit refactor phase in the transcript. The Notes say 'No external libraries used; implementation relies only on Python built-ins' but never considers or discusses replacing the manual loop with Python's built-in `sum()`.

expectations.text: Test names describe the behavioural rule being tested (e.g. 'empty_list_sums_to_zero', 'single_element_list_sums_to_that_element') rather than just the inputs
expectations.evidence: Test names are `test_empty_list`, `test_single_element`, `test_two_elements`, `test_multiple_elements`, `test_negative_numbers`, `test_mixed_positive_and_negative`, `test_large_list` — these describe the input shape, not the behavioural rule (e.g. no `_sums_to_zero` or `_sums_to_that_element` suffixes).

expectations.text: The final implementation is O(n) — no nested iteration or quadratic behaviour is introduced
expectations.evidence: Final implementation uses a single `for n in numbers` loop with constant-time accumulation. No nested loops or quadratic behaviour.

expectations.text: A test list or plan is described before implementation starts

expectations.text: All tests pass with the final implementation

expectations.text: The refactor phase removes any duplication introduced while making tests pass
expectations.evidence: Each cycle includes a REFACTOR section. No duplication was introduced at any stage; each GREEN step produced clean, minimal code. The final implementation uses a single `normalized` variable with no repeated logic.

expectations.text: The agent runs the test suite after each Red and Green phase
expectations.evidence: Every cycle shows pytest output in both RED (failure) and GREEN (pass) phases. Cycles 2 and 3 show a single run where the test passed immediately (no code change), which is noted explicitly — the suite was still run.

expectations.text: Forcing tests drive each new transformation step; the agent does not jump directly to the complete normalisation logic (e.g. s == s[::-1]) without intermediate tests forcing each addition
expectations.evidence: Transformations were incremental: Cycle 1 → constant True; Cycle 4 → s == s[::-1] (forced by non-palindrome test); Cycle 5 → added .lower() (forced by mixed-case test); Cycle 6 → added .isalnum() filter (forced by spaces/punctuation test). Full normalization was not written speculatively.

expectations.text: Triangulation tests (additional examples of the same rule) are added after the corresponding forcing test is green, not batched into the initial test list
expectations.evidence: No triangulation tests were added at any point in the transcript. The Step 0 plan explicitly states 'Triangulation tests are NOT listed here', but none were ever introduced in subsequent cycles either. The assertion cannot be satisfied when triangulation is absent entirely.

expectations.text: Test names describe the behaviour being verified (e.g. 'single_character_is_a_palindrome', 'ignores_case_when_comparing')
expectations.evidence: All test names clearly describe behavior: test_empty_string_is_palindrome, test_single_character_is_palindrome, test_simple_palindrome_is_palindrome, test_simple_non_palindrome_is_not_palindrome, test_mixed_case_palindrome_is_palindrome, test_palindrome_with_spaces_and_punctuation.

expectations.text: The implementation handles the full normalisation (case folding and non-alphanumeric stripping) only once tests require it — not speculatively
expectations.evidence: Case folding (.lower()) was added only in Cycle 5 when 'Aba' forced it. Non-alphanumeric stripping (.isalnum()) was added only in Cycle 6 when 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama' forced it. Neither was present before the forcing test.

expectations.text: A test list or plan is described before implementation starts
expectations.evidence: Step 1 (RED) lists the test categories to be covered (simple palindrome/non-palindrome, case insensitivity, ignoring spaces/punctuation, edge cases) before any implementation file is created in Step 2.

expectations.text: Tests cover: empty string, single character, simple palindrome, simple non-palindrome, mixed-case palindrome, palindrome with spaces or punctuation
expectations.evidence: test_empty_string, test_single_character, test_simple_palindrome, test_simple_non_palindrome, test_case_insensitive ('Racecar', 'MadAm'), test_ignore_spaces ('A man a plan a canal Panama'), and test_ignore_punctuation ('A man, a plan, a canal: Panama') are all present.

expectations.text: All tests pass with the final implementation
expectations.evidence: Transcript records '11 passed in 0.01s' after the GREEN phase implementation.

expectations.text: The refactor phase removes any duplication introduced while making tests pass
expectations.evidence: The implementation went straight to a clean single-expression form, so no duplication existed. The refactor phase correctly identifies nothing to change. No regression introduced.

expectations.text: The agent runs the test suite after each Red and Green phase
expectations.evidence: RED phase: 'ModuleNotFoundError: No module named palindrome' shown. GREEN phase: '11 passed in 0.01s' shown. Both phases have explicit test run output.

expectations.text: Forcing tests drive each new transformation step; the agent does not jump directly to the complete normalisation logic (e.g. s == s[::-1]) without intermediate tests forcing each addition
expectations.evidence: All 11 tests were written at once in Step 1, and the implementation immediately used the complete normalisation: 'filtered = [c.lower() for c in s if c.isalnum()]; return filtered == filtered[::-1]'. There were no incremental transformations — no minimal stub, no intermediate steps driven by failing tests one at a time.

expectations.text: Triangulation tests (additional examples of the same rule) are added after the corresponding forcing test is green, not batched into the initial test list
expectations.evidence: All 11 tests were batched into the initial test list before any implementation existed. For example, test_ignore_spaces and test_ignore_punctuation (two examples of the same stripping rule) were both written upfront in the RED phase rather than after the first stripping test was green.

expectations.text: Test names describe the behaviour being verified (e.g. 'single_character_is_a_palindrome', 'ignores_case_when_comparing')
expectations.evidence: Most test names describe the input, not the expected behaviour: test_empty_string, test_single_character, test_two_same_chars, test_two_different_chars, test_numbers, test_mixed_alphanum. Names like 'single_character_is_a_palindrome' or 'empty_string_is_a_palindrome' are absent. Only test_case_insensitive and test_ignore_spaces/test_ignore_punctuation adequately describe behaviour.

expectations.text: The implementation handles the full normalisation (case folding and non-alphanumeric stripping) only once tests require it — not speculatively
expectations.evidence: The agent wrote all normalisation-requiring tests simultaneously (including case and punctuation) and then immediately implemented the complete normalisation in one go. There was no incremental progression where a simpler implementation was first written and later extended as new tests demanded it.

notes: with_skill: 40/42 assertions pass (95.2%); baseline: 30/42 (71.4%) — delta +23.8 pp
bug-fix is a draw: both configurations score 7/7 (100%) — assertions may not strongly discriminate here
stack and palindrome show the largest gaps: with_skill +33 pp on stack, +33 pp on palindrome
Assertion 'tests added one at a time' and 'Stack not scaffolded upfront' are highly discriminating — baseline fails both
Baseline palindrome: all 11 tests written in one batch before any code, complete normalisation implemented speculatively
with_skill palindrome: no triangulation tests added — this expectation may be too constraining given TDD naturally produces them
The new iteration-2 evals (9 assertions per eval vs 5 in iteration-1) provide finer discrimination of TDD discipline
